objective.question: How do LPBF/SLM process parameters, thermal history, porosity, texture, and post-treatment affect densification, microstructure, mechanical, fatigue, corrosion, and wear behavior of 316L stainless steel?

paper_contributions.summary: P001 uses an SLM 316L process-parameter matrix to show how energy density, scanning speed, and scanning strategy affect relative density, yield strength, ultimate tensile strength, elongation, microhardness, and microstructure.
P002 compares preheating and non-preheating. Preheating changes cooling rate, melt pool geometry, porosity, yield strength, and elongation.
P003 links volumetric energy density with maximum defect diameter, LoF defects, fatigue limit, fatigue strength, and comparison with wrought 316L.
P004 shows heat treatment and HIP effects on residual stress, elongation, yield strength, hardness, density, and wear.
P005 shows that porosity affects corrosion in 3.5 wt.% NaCl through pitting potential, passive film resistance, passivation interval, and pore type.
P006 shows that scan rotation angle and build orientation change texture, ODF correlation, and yield strength prediction.

sample_result_matrix.summary: P001 preserves a 16-sample matrix. S014 reaches 99.45% relative density, 462.02 MPa yield strength, 584.44 MPa ultimate tensile strength, 41.9% elongation, and 223.4 HV microhardness.

controlled_comparisons.summary: P002 150 C preheating lowers cooling rate from 1.65e6 C/s to 1.43e6 C/s, increases melt-pool width/depth ratio from 1.38 to 1.70, increases yield strength from 448 MPa to 465 MPa, and increases elongation from 72% to 82%.

P003 shows density rising from 91.9% to 99.6% and maximum defect diameter dropping from 76 um to 50 um from low to high VED. The high-VED sample has lower yield strength, 437 MPa versus 462 MPa, and lower ultimate tensile strength, 560 MPa versus 610 MPa, but higher total elongation, 48.3% versus 33.2%, and higher fatigue strength at 10^4 cycles, 470 MPa versus 340 MPa.
P003 printed samples have fatigue limits around 80-100 MPa, below wrought 316L at 256 MPa.

P004 heat treatment and HIP reduce residual stress from 99.5 MPa to 17.8 MPa or 27.5 MPa. Under 120 W and 100 mm/s, yield strength drops from 464.8 MPa to 321.7 MPa or 322.1 MPa, while elongation rises from 35.0% to 51.2% or 52.2%.
P005 135 W-750 mm/s reaches 99.26% relative density and has the best corrosion resistance, with pitting potential 355.4 mV and passive-film resistance 1.90e5 ohm cm2.
P006 links scan rotation angle and build orientation to crystallographic texture, ODF, and yield strength prediction. Case 12 is uncertain, with ODF correlation 0.1195 and Jeffrey distance 2.2264.

mechanism_chains.path: energy input and scan strategy -> melt-pool stability and thermal accumulation -> porosity, LoF defects, balling, cellular or dendritic structure -> density, strength, ductility, hardness, fatigue
power and scan speed combination -> porosity level and pore type -> pitting initiation and passive-film stability -> pitting potential, passivation interval, Rfilm
scan rotation angle and build orientation -> crystallographic texture -> Taylor factor or Bishop-Hill response -> yield strength prediction

collection_conclusion.summary: LPBF/SLM 316L behavior is controlled by coupled energy input, scan strategy, thermal history, post-treatment, porosity, texture, and test environment rather than one process parameter alone. The collection supports different optimization paths for strength, ductility, fatigue, corrosion, wear, and yield-strength prediction.

limitations: P001 scan-speed units may be uncertain and should be rechecked before recalculation.
P001 relative density is SEM/ImageJ based and should not be treated as equivalent to Archimedes or micro-CT density.
P003 printed-vs-wrought comparisons have different processing histories and cannot be attributed only to VED.
P005 corrosion conclusions are limited to room-temperature 3.5 wt.% NaCl testing.
P006 is primarily a texture and yield-strength prediction paper, not a porosity-defect paper.
